summary: DHH argues that investing all your sense of purpose and happiness into a single area of life — whether work, hobbies, or friendships — leaves you dangerously exposed when that area inevitably hits trouble. He draws an analogy to financial diversification, suggesting people should spread their emotional investment across multiple pillars. The post warns against survivorship bias, noting that for every person who succeeds by going all-in, ninety-nine others crash and burn unseen. He advocates for resilience through balance, not necessarily equal distribution, but enough variety that no single setback can collapse your entire sense of self.
key_points: Staking all your happiness and drive on one thing — work, a hobby, or a social circle — makes you fragile when that thing fails
Life pillars can be weighted unevenly, but extreme concentration like 90/5/5 leaves you unprepared for inevitable setbacks
Survivorship bias distorts our view: we see the highlight reels of those who bet everything and won, never the majority who lost
Diversification isn't about equal distribution but about having enough backup pillars to stay standing when one crumbles
money_quotes: Betting your drive to get up in the morning on a single path will leave you completely stuck if that one road is blocked.
Don't bet all your happiness and purpose on a single square.
And planning for that by packing a bag with more than just hammers.
You'll only ever see their highlight reel, and you'll never see the other ninety-nine who crashed and burned.
It's fine to lean on one pillar or another at different times in your life, but you'd do well to make sure there's a few others holding up the roof if the first one crumbles.
key_insight: Resilience comes from distributing your sense of purpose across multiple areas of life so that no single setback can take everything down.
